content:
# Genesis Chapter 48 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 28r–28v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_048_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_048_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0052 (page 48), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0053 (page 49)

---

## Jacob Is Told Joseph Is Coming

**1** After these things, someone told Joseph, "Your father is ill." So Joseph took his two sons with him — Manasseh and Ephraim. **2** When Jacob was told, "Your son Joseph has come to you," Israel summoned his strength and sat up in bed.

---

## Jacob Recounts the Bethel Promise

**3** Jacob said to Joseph, "El Shaddai appeared to me at Luz in the land of Canaan and blessed me. **4** He said to me, 'I am going to make you fruitful and multiply you; I will make you into an assembly of peoples and give this land to your descendants after you as a possession forever.'

---

## The Adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh

**5** "And now: your two sons who were born to you in the land of Egypt before I came to you in Egypt — they are mine. Ephraim and Manasseh shall be mine just as Reuben and Simeon are mine. **6** But any children born to you after them shall be yours; they shall be counted under the names of their brothers in their inheritance.

---

## Rachel's Death — A Personal Remembrance

**7** "When I was coming from Paddan, Rachel died by my side in the land of Canaan, on the road, while there was still some distance to go to Ephrath — and I buried her there on the road to Ephrath, that is, Bethlehem."

---

## Jacob's Failing Eyes; He Claims the Sons

**8** Then Israel looked at Joseph's sons and said, "Who are these?" **9** Joseph said to his father, "They are my sons, whom God has given me here." And Jacob said, "Bring them to me now, so that I may bless them." **10** Now Israel's eyes had grown heavy with age — he could not see clearly. Joseph brought the boys close to him, and he kissed them and embraced them. **11** Israel said to Joseph, "I never expected to see your face again, and now God has let me see your children too." **12** Then Joseph drew them away from his knees and bowed down with his face to the ground.

---

## The Cross-Handed Blessing

**13** Joseph took them both — Ephraim on his right toward Israel's left, and Manasseh on his left toward Israel's right — and brought them close to him. **14** But Israel reached out his right hand and placed it on Ephraim's head, though he was the younger, and his left hand on Manasseh's head — deliberately crossing his hands, even though Manasseh was the firstborn.

---

## The Threefold Blessing

**15** He blessed Joseph and said:

"May the God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked —
the God who has shepherded me all my life long to this day,
**16** the angel who has redeemed me from all evil —
may he bless these boys.
Let my name be called upon them,
and the names of my fathers Abraham and Isaac.
And may they increase to a great multitude on the earth."

---

## Joseph Protests; Jacob Insists

**17** When Joseph saw that his father had placed his right hand on Ephraim's head, he was displeased, and he took hold of his father's hand to move it from Ephraim's head to Manasseh's head. **18** Joseph said to his father, "Not this way, my father — this one is the firstborn. Put your right hand on his head." **19** But his father refused and said, "I know, my son, I know. He too shall become a people, and he too shall be great. Yet his younger brother shall be greater than he, and his descendants shall fill the nations." **20** So he blessed them that day with this blessing:

"By you Israel will pronounce blessings, saying,
'May God make you as Ephraim and as Manasseh.'"

He put Ephraim before Manasseh.

---

## Jacob's Final Promise to Joseph

**21** Then Israel said to Joseph, "I am about to die, but God will be with you and will bring you back to the land of your fathers. **22** And I am giving you — over and above your brothers — one portion, which I took from the hand of the Amorites with my sword and my bow."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Sikkel et-Yadav — the Crossing of Hands as Prophetic Act

**וַיְשַׂכֵּל אֶת-יָדָיו** — *vayyaskel et-yadav* — "he deliberately crossed his hands."

This is one of the most carefully chosen words in the blessing narrative. The verb **שָׂכַל** (*sakhal*) does not mean "to err" or "to fumble"; it means to act with deliberate insight, with the shrewd intelligence of discernment — the same root as **מַשְׂכִּיל** (*maskil*), the wisdom-psalm designation, and **הַשְׂכֵּל** in the wisdom literature meaning "to act wisely, to understand." When the narrator says Jacob *sikkel* his hands, the text is making a precise claim: this is not the accidental blundering of a blind old man. This is not the tragic irony of Isaac, whose blindness was exploited by Jacob's disguise. This is a deliberate, knowing reversal — an act of prophetic intelligence, not bodily infirmity.

The echo of Genesis 27 is unmistakable and intentional. In that earlier scene, Jacob stood before his dying father Isaac wearing goat skins and impersonating Esau, receiving the firstborn blessing through deception while Isaac's failing eyes were the mechanism of the reversal. Isaac could not see; therefore the wrong son got the right blessing. The crossing of birthright and blessing in that chapter happened through trickery — Jacob's trickery, Rebekah's plan, Isaac's unknowing compliance. Now, in Genesis 48, the same pattern recurs: a dying patriarch, two sons, and the younger receiving the more prominent blessing over the older. But everything has changed. Jacob knows exactly what he is doing. He crosses his hands *with wisdom*, not by mistake. Joseph protests — tries to correct what he assumes is his old father's disorientation — and Jacob's reply is the pivot of the chapter: **יָדַעְתִּי בְנִי יָדַעְתִּי** (*yada'ti veni yada'ti*, "I know, my son, I know"). The repetition of *yada'ti* is emphatic and tender at once: the old man who had been deceived at his own deathbed blessing now stands on the other side of that story, as the one who knows, and chooses, and crosses his hands on purpose.

The history of biblical reversals — younger over older — has prepared for this moment. Abel over Cain. Isaac over Ishmael. Jacob over Esau. Joseph over his brothers. Now Ephraim over Manasseh. But this final instance in the Genesis line comes with a new element: the reversal is blessed, not stolen. The father does it with open eyes. The pattern is not broken by deception; it is fulfilled through prophetic sight. What was once a wound in Jacob's family becomes, in his hands, a gift.

---

### The Threefold Divine Title — Jacob's Final Theology

**הָאֱלֹהִים אֲשֶׁר הִתְהַלְּכוּ אֲבֹתַי לְפָנָיו... הָאֱלֹהִים הָרֹעֶה אֹתִי... הַמַּלְאָךְ הַגֹּאֵל אֹתִי**

"The God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked... the God who has shepherded me... the angel who has redeemed me."

The blessing in verses 15–16 is the most theologically sophisticated divine address in the entire book of Genesis. Jacob does not invoke God by name alone; he constructs, in three carefully balanced phrases, a comprehensive portrait of who God has been across the whole sweep of his life. This is Jacob's final theology — the accumulated understanding of a man who has met God at Bethel, wrestled with him at the Jabbok, been guided through famine and exile and reunion, and now lies on his deathbed with the thread of the entire story visible in retrospect.

The first phrase — **הָאֱלֹהִים אֲשֶׁר הִתְהַלְּכוּ אֲבֹתַי לְפָנָיו אַבְרָהָם וְיִצְחָק** (*ha-Elohim asher hit'halechu avotai lefanav Avraham v'Yitschak*, "the God before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac walked") — is retrospective and relational. **הִתְהַלֵּךְ** (*hit'halekh*, the reflexive-intensive form of *halakh*, to walk) carries the connotation of habitual, ongoing walking — not a single journey but a life's posture of walking before God. This is the term used of Enoch in Genesis 5:22 (*vayyit'halekh Hanokh et-ha-Elohim*, "and Enoch walked with God") and of Noah in Genesis 6:9. Jacob is locating himself in a lineage of those who have walked *before* God — not merely believed in God abstractly, but oriented their lives in continuous, sustained companionship with the divine presence.

The second phrase — **הָאֱלֹהִים הָרֹעֶה אֹתִי מֵעוֹדִי עַד-הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה** (*ha-Elohim ha-ro'eh oti me'odi ad-hayyom hazeh*, "the God who has shepherded me all my life to this day") — is remarkable for its choice of metaphor. Jacob is himself a shepherd; he spent twenty years as Laban's shepherd in Paddan-aram. He knows exactly what **רָעָה** (*ra'ah*, to shepherd) entails: to provide pasture, to seek the lost, to protect from predators, to carry the weak, to lead to water. Now, at the end of his life, he looks back and names God as the one who has done for him what he has spent his life doing for flocks. God has been his shepherd. Every turn of his story — the flight from Esau, the years in Haran, the return across the Jabbok, the grief over Joseph, the descent to Egypt — has been, Jacob now sees, a shepherd's guiding. The rod and the staff were present even when he could not see them. **מֵעוֹדִי עַד-הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה** (*me'odi ad-hayyom hazeh*, "from my very being to this day") — the phrase *me'odi* means literally "from my yet-being," from the first moment of my existence. God has been shepherding Jacob since before Jacob was formed into himself.

The third phrase — **הַמַּלְאָךְ הַגֹּאֵל אֹתִי מִכָּל-רָע** (*ha-mal'akh ha-go'el oti mikol-ra'*, "the angel who has redeemed me from all evil") — introduces a figure both distinct from and continuous with God. **הַמַּלְאָךְ** (*ha-mal'akh*, the angel, the messenger) is given the participle **הַגֹּאֵל** (*ha-go'el*), which is the technical term for the kinsman-redeemer — the next-of-kin who redeems land, ransoms slaves, and vindicates blood-debt. The go'el is not a distant benefactor; the go'el is *family*, the one with an obligation rooted in shared blood. For Jacob to call this angel his **גֹּאֵל** is to claim the most intimate possible relationship: this is my kinsman-redeemer, the one who is bound to me as family and who has exercised that bond in my redemption from every evil. The range of the word **רָע** (*ra'*, evil, harm, danger, misfortune, wickedness) is deliberately comprehensive: from all of it — Laban's exploitation, Esau's fury, Simeon and Levi's violence at Shechem, the loss of Joseph, the famine, the terror of sending Benjamin — from all of it, the redeeming angel has brought him through.

The three phrases together form a theological summation that moves from covenant history (the God of the fathers) to personal biography (the God who shepherded me) to immediate rescue (the angel who redeemed me). They move from general to specific, from collective to individual, from past continuity to present crisis. Jacob is, at the end of his life, naming the whole of what God has been — not in a creed but in a blessing.

---

### Rachel in the Middle — Why Now?

**וַאֲנִי בְּבֹאִי מִפַּדָּן מֵתָה עָלַי רָחֵל** — "And as for me, when I was coming from Paddan, Rachel died on me."

The intrusion of verse 7 — Rachel's death, inserted between the formal declaration of adoption (vv. 5–6) and the sight of the boys (v. 8) — is one of the most psychologically revealing moments in Genesis. There is no grammatical necessity for it here. The adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh as Jacob's sons is a legal-covenantal act that requires no reference to their grandmother. And yet Jacob interrupts himself to speak of Rachel.

The phrase **מֵתָה עָלַי** (*metah alai*, literally "she died upon me") is hauntingly personal. The preposition **עַל** (*al*, upon, against, over) in this construction conveys the weight of the death pressing down on the survivor — not merely "she died" as a neutral report but "she died *on me*," bearing down on my existence, leaving a mark I still carry. Jacob has never recovered from Rachel's death. He buried her on the road — **בְּדֶרֶךְ אֶפְרָת** (*b'derekh Efrat*, on the road to Ephrath) — not in the family tomb at Machpelah where Abraham and Sarah, Isaac and Rebekah are buried. She died in transit, in birth-labor, and Jacob could only erect a pillar (*massebah*) over her grave on the roadside.

Now Joseph's sons stand before him — Joseph, who was Rachel's firstborn, her first miracle after years of barrenness, the child she had demanded of God (*tena-li vanim*, "give me children, or I will die," Genesis 30:1). Joseph is Rachel in Jacob's eyes. And now here are Joseph's sons — Ephraim and Manasseh — Rachel's grandsons through the beloved son. The mention of Rachel at this moment is not a non sequitur; it is the emotional key to the entire scene. Jacob is not simply performing a legal adoption. He is, in the deepest register of his heart, reaching across the death of Rachel to gather her grandchildren into his arms — to give them the tribal standing that would have been given to her children had she lived long enough to have more. The adoption is an act of love extended backward through time to the woman who died on the road to Ephrath.

The place-name is also significant: **אֶפְרָת הִיא בֵּית לָחֶם** (*Efrat hi Beit Lekhem*, "Ephrath, that is, Bethlehem"). Jacob identifies the site with both its older name and its common name. Bethlehem — the house of bread — will become one of the most charged place-names in the entire biblical story: it is the city of Boaz and Ruth (Ruth 1:19), the city of David (1 Samuel 16:4), the city of the promised shepherd-king (Micah 5:2). Rachel's tomb on the outskirts of Bethlehem will be the place from which Jeremiah hears her weeping for her children (Jeremiah 31:15). The insertion of Bethlehem into this verse is a landmark — a signal planted in the text that the road through grief leads somewhere, that even the most desolate burial site on the most exposed road will one day be a named and remembered place.

---

### "The Angel Who Has Redeemed Me" — Go'el as Divine Title

**הַמַּלְאָךְ הַגֹּאֵל אֹתִי מִכָּל-רָע** — the angel, the go'el, who redeems me from all evil.

The term **גֹּאֵל** (*go'el*, kinsman-redeemer) is one of the richest legal-theological terms in the Hebrew Bible. In Israelite law, the go'el was the nearest male relative whose covenantal obligation included: (1) redeeming land sold from family patrimony (Leviticus 25:25–28); (2) ransoming a kinsman sold into slavery (Leviticus 25:47–49); (3) marrying a childless widow to preserve the family line (the levirate obligation, Deuteronomy 25:5–10, and Ruth 3–4); and (4) executing justice for a murdered kinsman, the **גֹּאֵל הַדָּם** (*go'el ha-dam*, the avenger of blood, Numbers 35:19). The go'el-concept thus encompasses rescue, preservation, redemption, and justice — all flowing from the obligation of kinship, of shared blood.

When Jacob calls the angel his go'el, he is deploying this legal term theologically with startling boldness. The angel is not merely a helper or a guardian spirit; the angel is *family*, bound by the covenantal obligations of kinship, obligated to rescue Jacob as a kinsman-redeemer is obligated to rescue an enslaved relative. This is an extraordinary claim: that the divine presence that has accompanied Jacob throughout his life has been functioning in the role of the nearest relative — tracking down the enslaved one, paying the ransom, recovering the lost patrimony, vindicating the blood.

The use of **גֹּאֵל** here anticipates its later theological development into one of the great divine titles. In the book of Job, Job cries out from his suffering: **וְאָנִי יָדַעְתִּי גֹּאֲלִי חָי** (*ve'ani yada'ti go'ali chai*, "and as for me, I know that my go'el lives," Job 19:25) — the same legal-relational term, now applied to God as the one who will vindicate Job in the face of death. In Isaiah 40–55, God is repeatedly called **גֹּאֵל יִשְׂרָאֵל** (*go'el Yisrael*, "the kinsman-redeemer of Israel") — the one who will buy Israel back from Babylonian exile as a go'el buys back sold land (Isaiah 41:14; 43:14; 44:6, 24; 48:17). The theological trajectory of the go'el runs from Jacob's deathbed blessing through Job's crisis through Isaiah's exile-redemption and into the New Testament, where the concept of redemption through a mediator-kinsman becomes the central metaphor for what Jesus accomplishes. Jacob's threefold blessing at the end of Genesis plants a seed that the prophets and apostles will spend centuries unfolding.

---

### Shechem Echad — The Wordplay That Ends Genesis

**וַאֲנִי נָתַתִּי לְךָ שְׁכֶם אַחַד עַל-אַחֶיךָ** — "And I am giving you one *shechem* above your brothers."

The final verse of Genesis 48 contains one of the most layered wordplays in the Hebrew Bible. **שְׁכֶם** (*shechem*) means simultaneously: (1) a *shoulder* (the anatomical part), hence by extension a *slope* or *portion* (a share of territory); and (2) the city of Shechem itself, the most significant Canaanite city in the central highlands of Canaan. Both meanings are active here, and the text does not resolve them — it holds them in creative tension.

Read as "one portion": Jacob is giving Joseph an extra share of the inheritance — a double portion beyond what each of the other tribes receives. This fits the context of the adoption: by making Ephraim and Manasseh his own sons, Jacob has effectively given Joseph two tribes instead of one, a double inheritance. "One *shechem* above your brothers" = "one extra shoulder-load of inheritance beyond what the others receive."

Read as "Shechem the city": Jacob is bequeathing the specific territory of Shechem to Joseph. In Joshua 24:32, Joseph's bones (brought up from Egypt, Genesis 50:25) will be buried at Shechem, in the plot of ground that Jacob had bought from the sons of Hamor (Genesis 33:19–20). The city of Shechem will become the seat of the Joseph tribes — it falls within Manasseh's territory (Joshua 17:7) and is designated a city of refuge (Joshua 20:7; 21:21). The claim that Jacob *took* the Shechem territory "with my sword and my bow" from the Amorites is not corroborated elsewhere in Genesis (Genesis 33:18–20 records that Jacob *purchased* the land, and the violent episode of Genesis 34 involved Simeon and Levi's massacre, not Jacob's). Whether this refers to a tradition not preserved in Genesis 34, or whether Jacob is reappropriating the deed of his sons (however much he condemned it), or whether **חֶרֶב וְקַשְׁתִּי** (*khervi v'kashti*, "my sword and my bow") functions idiomatically for asserting legal conquest — the verse remains one of the most enigmatic in the chapter.

What is theologically clear is the movement: Ephraim and Manasseh, born in Egypt, children of the diaspora, will become the central hill-country tribes of Israel. The Joseph tribes will inherit the heartland — the ridge route, the mountain spine of Canaan, the territory that will eventually divide into the northern kingdom of Israel with Shechem as one of its founding cities. The bequeathing of *shechem* to Joseph is the bequeathing of the center, the inheritance of the place where the covenant story in Canaan was always destined to land.

---

## Manuscript Observations and LXX Variants

**Vaticanus page structure:** Vaticanus page pa_0048 (GEN_048_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) is marked with "42" at the upper left (chapter or section number in the Greek manuscript's sectional division). The page carries text continuing from the end of Genesis 47 and opening Genesis 48 in the three-column uncial format characteristic of Vaticanus throughout Genesis. The dense uncial script is clearly legible across all three columns, with no illuminated initials in the Genesis section — Vaticanus reserves decorative display for major book divisions. Page pa_0049 (GEN_048_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) shows at its upper left the marking **ΓΑΡ ΙΙΙ** (or similar), indicating the continuation of the chapter. The Genesis 48 text in Vaticanus exhibits several expansions relative to the MT, particularly in the blessing narrative.

**The Bethel theophany (v. 3-4):** For **וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלַי הִנְנִי מַפְרְךָ וְהִרְבִּיתִיךָ** ("He said to me, 'Behold, I will make you fruitful and multiply you'"), the LXX preserves this closely: **καὶ εἶπέν μοι ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ αὐξανῶ σε καὶ πληθυνῶ σε** — "and he said to me, 'Behold, I will increase you and multiply you.'" The MT's **מַפְרְךָ** (*mafrekha*, "making you fruitful," from *parah*) is rendered **αὐξανῶ** (*auxanō*, "I will increase/grow"), the same verb used in the creation blessing of Genesis 1:28 in the LXX — **αὐξάνεσθε καὶ πληθύνεσθε** (*auxanesthe kai plēthynesthe*, "increase and multiply"). The LXX translators thus connect Jacob's blessing to the Adamic creation mandate.

**"Perpetual possession" (v. 4):** For **לַאֲחֻזַּת עוֹלָם** (*la'achuzzat olam*, "for a perpetual possession"), the LXX reads **εἰς κατάσχεσιν αἰώνιον** — "for an eternal holding/possession." The Greek **κατάσχεσις** (*kataschesis*, holding, possession, inheritance) is the LXX's standard term for the covenantal landholding. The term **αἰώνιον** (*aiōnion*, eternal/age-long) translates **עוֹלָם** (*olam*), which means not merely "permanent" but "belonging to the olam" — to the world-age, the indefinitely extended time-span of the covenant.

**The adoption declaration (v. 5):** The LXX adds a significant plus in verse 5. After "Ephraim and Manasseh shall be mine just as Reuben and Simeon are mine," the LXX inserts: **καὶ ἡ ἔκγονος σου ἣν ἐγέννησας μετ' αὐτοὺς σοί ἔσονται** — "and your offspring whom you have begotten after them shall be yours" — which corresponds roughly to the MT's verse 6. But the LXX also preserves a note that **εἰς τὴν ἐμὴν κληρονομίαν κληρονομήσουσιν** — "they shall inherit in my inheritance" — sharpening the legal precision of the adoption language. The Greek **κληρονομία** (*klēronomia*, inheritance) is a technical term from Greek inheritance law, precisely the right word for what Jacob is doing in legally transferring tribal status.

**"Who are these?" (v. 8):** The LXX's rendering of Jacob's question — **τίνες οὗτοί σοι εἰσίν** (*tines houtoi soi eisin*, "who are these to you?") — preserves the dative *soi* (to you, for you) that the MT carries in **מִי-אֵלֶּה** (*mi-eileh*, "who are these?"). The LXX adds the relational anchor, making the question slightly more specific: not just "who are they" but "who are these who belong to you?" — though this may be the translator's expansion rather than a variant Hebrew text.

**Sikkel et-yadav (v. 14):** For **וַיְשַׂכֵּל אֶת-יָדָיו** (*vayyaskel et-yadav*, "he deliberately crossed his hands"), the LXX reads **ἀλλάξας τὰς χεῖρας** (*allaxas tas cheiras*, "having exchanged/crossed his hands") — the Greek participle from **ἀλλάσσω** (*allassō*, to change, exchange, alter). This is a reasonable rendering of the physical action but loses entirely the wisdom-connotation of **שָׂכַל** (*sakhal*, to act with discernment, to understand wisely). The LXX gives us the gesture; the Hebrew gives us the meaning. The MT's choice of *sakhal* — the wisdom verb — signals intentionality; the LXX's *allaxas* signals only the physical fact of the crossing. Translators who follow the LXX tradition here may inadvertently suggest Jacob made an accidental or merely physical gesture, whereas the Hebrew is at pains to say this was a knowing act.

**The threefold blessing (vv. 15-16):** The Vaticanus text of the blessing is carefully preserved and closely aligned with the MT in structure. The LXX renders **הָרֹעֶה אֹתִי** (*ha-ro'eh oti*, "who shepherded me") as **ὁ τρέφων με** (*ho trephōn me*, "who has nourished/fed me") — using *trephō* (to nourish, feed, rear) rather than the standard shepherd term *poimainō*. This is a notable choice: *trephō* is used for nursing mothers (1 Thessalonians 2:7), for feeding young animals, for the rearing of children. The LXX translator has chosen a more intimate and nurturing term — not the professional shepherd managing a flock, but the parent or nurse providing sustenance to the dependent young. Both images are theologically profound; they emphasize different facets of God's care.

For **הַגֹּאֵל אֹתִי מִכָּל-רָע** (*ha-go'el oti mikol-ra'*, "who redeems me from all evil"), the LXX reads **ὁ ῥυόμενός με ἐκ πάντων τῶν κακῶν** — "who delivers me from all evils." The Greek **ῥύομαι** (*rhyomai*, to rescue, deliver, snatch from danger) is a strong rescue verb — it appears in Matthew 6:13 in the Lord's Prayer: **ῥῦσαι ἡμᾶς ἀπὸ τοῦ πονηροῦ** ("deliver us from evil"). The LXX's choice of *rhyomai* for *go'el* here captures the rescue-dimension of the kinsman-redeemer but loses the kinship-obligation dimension that makes *go'el* so theologically specific. The MT's *go'el* implies not merely that God rescues but that God rescues as a *relative*, bound by covenant-kinship to do so.

**"I know, my son, I know" (v. 19):** For **יָדַעְתִּי בְנִי יָדַעְתִּי** (*yada'ti veni yada'ti*), the LXX reads **οἶδα τέκνον οἶδα** (*oida teknon oida*) — "I know, child, I know." The Greek preserves the repetition and the affectionate address (*teknon*, child/dear one) faithfully. The doubled **οἶδα** (*oida*, I know) matches the doubled **יָדַעְתִּי** (*yada'ti*) with precision.

---

## Structural Note

Genesis 48 is built around a chiastic architecture of reversal and recognition, with the blessing of vv. 15–16 as its apex:

**A — The Approach (vv. 1–2):** Joseph comes to the dying Jacob; Jacob summons his strength to receive him.

**B — The Legal Foundation: Adoption (vv. 3–7):** Jacob grounds the adoption in the Bethel theophany and in covenant promise. The Rachel reminiscence (v. 7) inserts the emotional logic beneath the legal act.

**C — The Visual Limit and the Embrace (vv. 8–12):** Jacob cannot see clearly — but he kisses and embraces the boys. Sight is limited; touch and presence are not. Jacob bows.

**D — The Cross-Handed Positioning (vv. 13–14):** Joseph positions the boys to receive the natural blessing order. Jacob crosses his hands — the reversal is set.

**E — THE BLESSING: Threefold Divine Title (vv. 15–16):** The theological center and summit of the chapter. Jacob speaks his final theology: God-of-the-fathers, Shepherd-God, Redeemer-Angel.

**D' — The Challenge and Insistence (vv. 17–20):** Joseph tries to correct the reversal. Jacob insists: "I know, my son, I know." The blessing formula seals it.

**C' — Death Announced, Continuity Promised (v. 21):** Jacob's sight of the future is clear though his physical eyes fail. "God will be with you."

**B' — Legal Completion: The Extra Portion (v. 22):** The legal act is sealed — Joseph receives *shechem*, the extra share, grounding the future tribal inheritance in present covenantal act.

**A' — (Implied departure; the scene closes.)**

The name-alternation in this chapter is especially precise and purposeful. The narrator uses **יַעֲקֹב** (*Ya'akov*, Jacob) when the patriarch speaks in the personal-familial mode: Jacob recounts Rachel's death (v. 7), Jacob speaks to Joseph (v. 11), Jacob sees Joseph's sons (v. 8). The narrator uses **יִשְׂרָאֵל** (*Yisrael*, Israel) when the patriarch acts in the covenantal-institutional mode: Israel sat up in bed to receive his son (v. 2), Israel reached out his right hand to bless (v. 14), Israel blessed Joseph (v. 15), Israel said to Joseph (v. 21). The dying man who crosses his hands is Israel — the nation in seed form, acting with the authority of a founding patriarch to shape the future. The man who weeps over Rachel is Jacob — the human being who loved and lost and carries the memory. Both act in this chapter. The blessing emerges from the meeting of both: from the personal grief (Rachel died; therefore I adopt her grandsons) and the covenantal authority (Israel crosses his hands deliberately, prophetically, finally).

The chapter's position in the Genesis narrative is also structurally critical. Genesis 47:31 closed with Jacob bowing on the *rosh ha-mittah* — the head of the bed — in response to Joseph's oath. Genesis 48:1 opens with Jacob in bed, ill, about to die. Genesis 49 will give us the great blessing of all twelve sons. Genesis 48 is the threshold: the private, intimate scene between Jacob and Joseph before the public declaration of chapter 49. Here, in the semi-darkness of a sick room, with an old man's failing eyes and a young man's misread gesture, the twelve-tribe structure of Israel is finalized. Ephraim and Manasseh replace Joseph in the tribal roster — Joseph will have no tribe named Joseph; instead, his double portion is expressed through the double tribeship of his sons. The adoption scene of Genesis 48 is the legal instrument that makes the twelve-tribe configuration possible.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 28r–28v, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Folio 28r (GEN_048_page_001.jpg) carries Genesis 48:1 through approximately verse 12, covering the opening narrative, the adoption declaration, Rachel's death remembrance, and the beginning of the blessing approach. Folio 28v (GEN_048_page_002.jpg) carries the remainder of the chapter from approximately verse 13 through verse 22 — the cross-handed blessing, the threefold divine title, Joseph's protest and Jacob's insistence, the blessing formula, and the final bequest of shechem. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 48–49 (pa_0048–pa_0049); accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0052 and https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0053.*
